title: Correcting a Faulty Sieve of Eratosthenes

statement.markdown: Implement the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm to find all prime numbers up to `n`. The algorithm should handle edge cases where `n` is very large and efficiently use space, producing the correct result for large inputs.
statement.inputFormat: Input is a single integer `n` (1 <= n <= 2 * 10^5).
statement.outputFormat: Output is a boolean array, where `arr[i]` represents whether `i` is a prime number or not.
statement.notes: The algorithm should have a time complexity of O(n log log n) and space complexity of O(n).

examples.output: false true true false false true false false false false
examples.explanation: The prime numbers up to 10 are 2, 3, 5, 7.

hints.text: First hint text: Use a boolean array to efficiently represent prime numbers.

editorial.title: Sieve of Eratosthenes Algorithm
editorial.intuition: The algorithm works by iteratively marking the multiples of each prime number starting from 2.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would check for divisibility of each number from 2 to n.
editorial.betterSolution: The Sieve of Eratosthenes improves upon the brute force approach by only considering the prime numbers.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves creating a boolean array and marking the multiples of each prime number.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The algorithm is correct as it iteratively removes composite numbers from the list.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Start with a boolean array initialized to true. Then, iterate over the array and mark the multiples of each prime number.
